title: Resurgent Inflation Forces Hawkish Fed Pivot and Higher-for-Longer Repricing
content: Inflation surprised persistently to the upside through the window, forcing a hawkish repricing that dominated rates, FX and equity-sector leadership. Headline CPI accelerated, with April at 3.8% YoY and a hotter May print near 4.2%, the strongest since 2023, while PPI jumped around 6.5% on a near-11% energy surge. At his first meeting as chair, Kevin Warsh led a unanimous hold at 3.50-3.75% but stripped the 2026 cut from the dot plot, the median now implying at least one hike and nine of eighteen officials penciling tightening. Long-dated USTs sold off, the 30-year cracking 5% to a near-two-decade high and the 2-year holding cycle highs above 4%; the USD firmed. Strong retail sales reinforced the higher-for-longer regime, pressuring rate-sensitive sectors even as broad indices held near records.

title: Israel-Iran Conflict Drives Volatile Oil-Led Geopolitical Risk Premium
content: Middle East tensions were the principal swing factor for cross-asset volatility, injecting and then unwinding a sizeable energy risk premium. Crude spiked when Israel struck Iran in mid-June, lifting headline inflation fears, bidding safe-haven gold and USTs, and pressuring risk assets on concerns over the Strait of Hormuz. The premium reversed sharply late in the window as a US-Iran framework agreement emerged, raising hopes of de-escalation and unimpeded shipping; oil slumped around 13% toward $85 a barrel, easing inflation anxiety and allowing long-end yields to fall. The whiplash kept FX, commodity and equity-index direction hostage to headlines, fed directly into the inflation narrative through energy prices, and saw energy stocks lag sharply, down nearly 6% on the week, even as falling crude supported the broader tape into the close.

title: AI Semiconductor Leadership Drives Index Gains Amid Sharp Valuation Swings
content: AI-driven semiconductor leadership remained the dominant equity narrative, but with sharply rising two-way volatility around stretched valuations. Earnings momentum stayed exceptional, with reported S&P 500 profits up roughly 30% YoY led by technology, and Nvidia reclaimed a $5-trillion market capitalisation, hitting a record near $217 as the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index pushed to fresh highs. That concentration cut both ways: a mid-June session erased over $1.3 trillion as chipmakers tumbled, AMD off 7% and Intel 8%, on profit-taking and rate-driven valuation jitters, before a swift rebound led indices higher, the Nasdaq 100 jumping 2.5% on the week. The episode underscored how narrow leadership leaves benchmarks hostage to a handful of names, amplifying swings as higher-for-longer rates challenge rich multiples even as structural AI infrastructure demand underpins the secular bid.
